What Do You Do When You Realize You're Underskilled After Joining a New Job?

A few people in similar situations have come to chat with me about their recent work. The recurring theme: "After joining I realized my skills can't keep up at all, I can't contribute in meetings — should I just quit?"

The "I realized my skills can't keep up at all, I can't contribute in meetings — should I quit?" scenario commonly hits people in their first job, because we tend to romanticize the role. We imagine engineers earn high salaries, type fast, and watch lines of code fly across the screen looking impressive. Reality is often different.

I remember my first job after getting out of the military was writing C#. Honestly, the first language I'd learned was PHP, and during the interview no one mentioned the role used a different language — even the in-person coding test was in PHP — so I figured I'd be writing PHP at work.
I got the offer, fortunately. But that's when the trouble actually started.

On day one I was told I'd be switching to a different language: C#. Before pivoting to frontend, I'd never touched any language besides PHP. I was a rookie (admittedly), even though later I'd dabbled in Python, C, etc. (just surface-level — no depth).
So onboarding hit me hard, because I had zero experience with this language.

The company assigned a senior to mentor me, but they were busy with their own project too. We barely talked twice a week. So for 8 hours a day I was grinding to figure out C# and what the heck MVC was.
This job only lasted six months, but during it I had a
of dark thoughts — "Am I really cut out to be an engineer?", "Should I just give up?", "Should I resign at the end of probation?"

That first job hit me with serious anxiety — to the point my emotions almost broke a few times. The company originally planned to have someone in my position take over a major project, but because I had no C# experience, they handed the project to another colleague instead.

I was Googling like crazy about how to learn C#, but honestly, learning something completely new in a month is brutal — and I didn't even know what MVC was structurally. Plus, online learning resources were sparse back then, so I was mostly stuck with books. I was so green I didn't even know Git.

Under time pressure and skill gap, about a month in, I had my first real meeting. I felt completely powerless — I couldn't follow what my colleagues were saying. I just sat there hugging my head like a Psyduck. The anxiety had peaked, especially because I saw my colleague who'd taken over my project working nearly to burnout. I felt like garbage.
By the second month, I worked up the courage to face the anxiety and started adjusting mentally — and things began to improve.
At that point I didn't even know what direction or what keywords to Google. But I didn't want to quit, so I worked up the nerve to message my senior via internal chat:
"Senior, sorry to bother you — I'd like some pointers on how to learn C#. Could you give me some direction?"

I sat on that message for half an hour before sending. Everyone was on big projects, the office vibe was tense, I didn't want to interrupt. About 10 minutes after I sent it, no reply — just as my heart was sinking, they responded:
"Don't worry about how to learn C# yet — first understand MVC..."

That one courageous message led to them pointing me to books and resources, and my anxiety slowly started to decrease. The anxiety came from my lack of skill — and the reason I'd avoided facing the root was "I was afraid of bothering people" and "I didn't want to admit I sucked". That avoidance was generating all the negative energy.

Once I faced the anxiety and accepted "yeah, I suck right now", things shifted significantly. I started fearlessly asking my senior about everything — server setup, GCP operations, even started converting our company project from another language to C#.MVC.
The job only lasted six months, but I learned a lot. I slowly became able to discuss programming with my senior and other colleagues (still green, but the gap from day one was night-and-day).
Through this I learned: we generate negative thoughts and anxiety almost unconsciously, but if you never face the

for your anxiety, you just accumulate more negative energy and lean further toward quitting. So I stopped, adjusted my daily routine, and developed some weirdly mindless courage to ask seniors questions (looking back, everyone was busy — I'm sorry I kept interrupting).
By admitting I genuinely sucked, I went in expecting to be yelled at — though seniors never actually yelled. And the anxiety started receding.

In this job I really came to understand: when you notice negative energy accumulating, stop and rest. We often don't notice we've sunk into a negative loop. The moment you notice you're sleeping poorly, eating poorly, or feel like there's a weight on your chest because of something, you really should stop and think about

A bit of pressure helps you grow — but too much pressure leads to negative growth. You have to identify the source: is it skill gap? Self-imposed pressure? Once I started facing the anxiety, I asked myself: why did I come to this company? I worked hard to win this offer. Why am I letting myself drown in anxiety instead of confronting it?
There will always be people who look down on you, but their dismissal is more reason to push through. When you break through, they'll switch from looking down to looking up.

Also — when a company offers you a job, it means they're willing to invest in you and trust you. They saw something specific in you. Even if you only stay for the 3-month probation, that probation can change the rest of your career if you use it well. Because once you see reality firsthand, you'll have direction for where to grow next.

I genuinely hope this post gives you a bit of encouragement. You're not alone — I went through the same thing. I really hope you don't let temporary negative thoughts lead to regret you can't take back.
